medical_entities.angina: chest pain due to reduced blood flow
medical_entities.antibiotics: medicines used to treat bacterial infections
medical_entities.antihistamines: medicines that reduce or block allergic reactions
medical_entities.chemotherapy: treatment using drugs to kill cancer cells
medical_entities.cyanosis: bluish skin due to low oxygen
medical_entities.diabetes mellitus: chronic condition causing high blood sugar
medical_entities.radiation therapy: treatment using radiation to kill cancer cells

simplification.wbc: white blood cells that are part of the immune system and help the body fight infections and foreign invaders
simplification.rbc: red blood cells that carry oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body and bring carbon dioxide back to the lungs
simplification.urea: a waste product formed in the liver from protein breakdown and removed from the body by the kidneys through urine
simplification.basophils: a type of white blood cell involved in allergic reactions and inflammation, helping release chemicals like histamine
simplification.monocytes: a type of white blood cell that helps fight infections by engulfing and destroying pathogens and dead cells
simplification.neutrophils: the most abundant type of white blood cell that acts as the first line of defense by attacking bacteria and infections
simplification.eosinophils: a type of white blood cell that plays a role in fighting parasitic infections and is also involved in allergic responses
simplification.lymphocytes: a type of white blood cell that includes T cells and B cells, which are essential for immune response and antibody production
simplification.hypertension: a condition where the force of blood against artery walls is consistently too high, which can increase the risk of heart disease, stroke, and kidney problems
simplification.hypotension: a condition where blood pressure is lower than normal, which may cause dizziness, fainting, or shock in severe cases
simplification.diabetes mellitus: a long-term condition where the body cannot properly control blood sugar levels due to lack of insulin or resistance to it
simplification.hyperglycemia: a condition where blood sugar levels are higher than normal, often seen in diabetes and can cause damage to organs over time
simplification.hypoglycemia: a condition where blood sugar levels drop too low, leading to symptoms like sweating, confusion, and fainting
simplification.anemia: a condition in which the body does not have enough healthy red blood cells to carry oxygen, causing fatigue and weakness
simplification.asthma: a chronic condition in which the airways become inflamed and narrow, making it difficult to breathe and causing wheezing or coughing
simplification.copd: a progressive lung disease that blocks airflow and makes breathing difficult, often caused by smoking or long-term exposure to irritants
simplification.pneumonia: an infection of the lungs that causes inflammation of air sacs, which may fill with fluid or pus and lead to coughing, fever, and breathing difficulty
simplification.bronchitis: inflammation of the bronchial tubes that carry air to the lungs, often causing cough with mucus and breathing discomfort
simplification.dyspnea: a feeling of shortness of breath or difficulty breathing, often associated with lung or heart conditions
simplification.tachycardia: a condition where the heart beats faster than normal, which may reduce its efficiency in pumping blood
simplification.bradycardia: a condition where the heart beats slower than normal, which can cause dizziness or fatigue
simplification.arrhythmia: a condition where the heart beats in an irregular rhythm, either too fast, too slow, or unevenly
simplification.angina: chest pain or discomfort that occurs when the heart muscle does not receive enough oxygen-rich blood
simplification.myocardial infarction: a serious condition commonly known as a heart attack, where blood flow to a part of the heart is blocked, causing damage to heart muscle
simplification.stroke: a medical emergency where blood supply to part of the brain is interrupted or reduced, leading to brain cell damage
simplification.edema: swelling caused by excess fluid trapped in the body's tissues, often seen in legs, feet, or lungs
simplification.cyanosis: a bluish discoloration of the skin or lips caused by low oxygen levels in the blood
simplification.fatigue: a feeling of extreme tiredness or lack of energy that does not improve with rest
simplification.fever: an increase in body temperature above normal, usually as a response to infection
simplification.nausea: an uneasy feeling in the stomach that often comes before vomiting
simplification.vomiting: the forceful expulsion of stomach contents through the mouth
simplification.diarrhea: frequent passage of loose or watery stools, often caused by infection or digestive issues
simplification.constipation: difficulty in passing stools, often due to slow bowel movement or lack of fiber
simplification.syncope: a temporary loss of consciousness, commonly known as fainting, usually caused by reduced blood flow to the brain
simplification.palpitations: a sensation where a person becomes aware of their heartbeat, often feeling it as racing, pounding, or irregular
simplification.migraine: a severe type of headache often accompanied by nausea, sensitivity to light, and visual disturbances
simplification.epilepsy: a neurological disorder characterized by repeated seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain
simplification.dementia: a group of conditions that affect memory, thinking, and social abilities severely enough to interfere with daily life
simplification.alzheimer's: a progressive brain disorder that gradually destroys memory and thinking skills
simplification.parkinson's: a disorder of the nervous system that affects movement, causing tremors, stiffness, and slow movement
simplification.arthritis: inflammation of one or more joints, causing pain, stiffness, and reduced movement
simplification.osteoporosis: a condition where bones become weak and brittle, increasing the risk of fractures
simplification.fracture: a break or crack in a bone, usually caused by injury or weakness in the bone
simplification.infection: invasion of the body by harmful microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, or fungi
simplification.inflammation: the body's natural response to injury or infection, causing redness, swelling, heat, and pain
simplification.cancer: a disease where abnormal cells grow uncontrollably and can spread to other parts of the body
simplification.tumor: an abnormal mass of tissue that can be benign (non-cancerous) or malignant (cancerous)
simplification.metastasis: the process by which cancer spreads from its original site to other parts of the body
simplification.chemotherapy: a treatment that uses strong drugs to destroy cancer cells or stop them from growing
simplification.radiation therapy: a treatment that uses high-energy radiation to kill or shrink cancer cells
simplification.biopsy: a medical test where a small sample of tissue is removed from the body to be examined for disease
simplification.insulin: a hormone produced by the pancreas that helps regulate blood sugar levels in the body
simplification.antibiotics: medicines used to treat infections caused by bacteria by killing or stopping their growth
simplification.analgesics: medications that relieve pain without causing loss of consciousness
simplification.antihistamines: medicines that reduce or block allergic reactions by affecting histamine levels
simplification.bp: blood pressure — the pressure of circulating blood on the walls of blood vessels
simplification.hr: the number of times the heart beats per minute, indicating cardiovascular activity
simplification.spo2: the percentage of oxygen present in the blood, indicating how well oxygen is being delivered to the body
simplification.ecg: a test that records the electrical activity of the heart to detect heart problems
simplification.mri: a medical imaging technique that uses magnetic fields to create detailed images of organs and tissues
simplification.ct scan: an imaging method that combines X-rays to create cross-sectional images of the body
simplification.cbc: a blood test that measures different components of blood such as red cells, white cells, and platelets
simplification.hemoglobin: a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body
simplification.creatinine: a waste product in the blood that indicates how well the kidneys are functioning
simplification.cholesterol: a fatty substance in the blood that is necessary for body functions but can be harmful in high levels

wbc.name: Total White Blood Cells
wbc.low: may indicate a weak immune system, viral infection, or bone marrow issues
wbc.high: may indicate infection, inflammation, or an immune response
wbc.precaution: consult a doctor, maintain hygiene, and follow proper medication if infection is suspected

rbc.name: Red Blood Cells
rbc.low: may indicate anemia, fatigue, or blood loss
rbc.high: may indicate dehydration or lung/heart conditions
rbc.precaution: maintain an iron-rich diet and consult a doctor if symptoms persist

hemoglobin.name: Hemoglobin
hemoglobin.low: may indicate anemia or nutritional deficiency
hemoglobin.high: may indicate dehydration or an underlying condition
hemoglobin.precaution: consume iron-rich foods such as spinach and lentils, and consult a doctor

platelet count.name: Platelets
platelet count.low: may cause bleeding issues and delayed clotting
platelet count.high: may increase risk of clot formation
platelet count.precaution: avoid injuries and consult a doctor immediately if abnormal

neutrophils.name: Neutrophils
neutrophils.low: may indicate weak immune defense or viral infection
neutrophils.high: may indicate bacterial infection or stress response
neutrophils.precaution: monitor for signs of infection and consult a doctor if abnormal

lymphocytes.name: Lymphocytes
lymphocytes.low: may indicate immune suppression or HIV
lymphocytes.high: may indicate viral infection or chronic inflammation
lymphocytes.precaution: maintain a healthy immune system and seek medical advice

monocytes.name: Monocytes
monocytes.high: may indicate chronic infection or inflammation
monocytes.precaution: consult a doctor for further diagnosis

eosinophils.name: Eosinophils
eosinophils.high: may indicate allergies, asthma, or parasitic infection
eosinophils.precaution: avoid known allergens and consult a doctor

basophils.name: Basophils
basophils.high: may indicate allergic reactions or inflammation
basophils.precaution: monitor allergies and consult a doctor if needed

urea.name: Urea
urea.low: may indicate liver issues or malnutrition
urea.high: may indicate kidney dysfunction or dehydration
urea.precaution: stay well-hydrated and consult a doctor

creatinine.name: Creatinine
creatinine.high: may indicate kidney problems or reduced kidney function
creatinine.precaution: monitor kidney health and consult a doctor
